        -   This will almost certainly be the hardest assignment; getting the basics down is the hard part!

## GGplot and the Grammar of Graphics

::: incremental
-   While it is possible to plot in base R (without loading a package), nearly everyone uses ggplot2 instead

-   Designed by Hadley Wickham, who introduces a "Grammar of Graphics"

-   A plot is composed of **three core elements** (and other options)

    -   **The Data**

    -   **The Aesthetic Mapping** of variables to visual cues

    -   The **Geometry** used to encode observations (the *type* of plot)
:::

## The ggplot Template

Every ggplot follows this basic pattern:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = <YOUR_DATA>,
      aes(<YOUR_MAPPINGS>)) +
  <GEOM_FUNCTION>()
```
:::


::: incremental
-   **data =** your dataframe (what you're plotting)
-   **aes()** = "aesthetic mappings" (which variables go where)
    -   `x =` what goes on the x-axis
    -   `y =` what goes on the y-axis
    -   `color/fill =` what determines point/line color
    -   other options, like `shape` etc
-   **geom\_** = the geometry (type of plot: points, bars, lines, etc.)
:::

## Scatter Plots

-   Useful for understanding the bivariate relationship (or joint distribution) between two variables

-   We often add a *line of fit* (linear or smoothed) to make the relationship more explicit

-   in ggplot, geom_point() tells R to make a scatter plot

## A simple example


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(ggplot2)
library(palmerpenguins)

ggplot(data = penguins,
      aes(x = bill_length_mm,
          y = bill_depth_mm,
          color = species)) +
    geom_point()
```
:::


## A simple example


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410_DataViz_Probability_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## Step 2: Add titles and labels

Just add two lines to make it readable:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = penguins,
       mapping = aes(x = bill_length_mm,
                     y = bill_depth_mm,
                     color = species)) +
    geom_point() +
    ggtitle("Penguin Bill Dimensions by Species") +
    labs(x = "Bill Length (mm)", y = "Bill Depth (mm)")
```
:::


## Step 2: Add titles and labels


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410_DataViz_Probability_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## Step 3: Make it publication-ready

Now we can add more polish:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = penguins,
       mapping = aes(x = bill_length_mm,
                     y = bill_depth_mm,
                     color = species)) +
    geom_point() +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
    ggtitle("Characteristics of Penguins by Species") +
    labs(x = "Bill Length (mm)", y = "Bill Depth (mm)") +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
          axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
          plot.title = element_text(size = 16, hjust = 0.5))
```
:::


## Step 3: Make it publication-ready


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410_DataViz_Probability_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## What is the Goal?

We want to convey information to the reader

::: incremental
-   clearly

-   efficiently

-   as simply as possible
:::

## Some Basic Rules

::: incremental
-   For most plots, we want the *outcome* or *dependent* variable on the **Y axis**

-   Similarly, the key *independent variable* should be on the **X axis**

-   Think carefully about axes. Use a single scale, with sensible values!

-   Clearly label plots. Rename variables so reader can understand
:::

## More Rules

::: incremental
-   Use colorblind friendly color schemes

    -   In R, the default scheme has improved.

    -   The [palette.colors](https://journal.r-project.org/articles/RJ-2023-071/) function returns even better color schemes

-   As a general rule (there may be exceptions)

    -   Don't use pie graphs, they are hard to interpret

    -   Similarly, 3D graphics rarely add anything to plots

-   If you want to track trends across different groups/subjects, consider *faceting.*
:::

## Load the ANES data

For the following examples, we'll use the American National Election Study (ANES) data.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(tidyverse)

# Load the ANES data (make sure this file is in your working directory)
anes <- read_csv("anes.csv")

# For now, we'll work with a subset to keep plots readable
anes_first200 <- anes[1:200,]  # take the first 200 rows
```
:::


Let's also drop respondents who didn't vote:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
anes_first200 <- anes_first200 |>
  filter(is.na(votechoice) == FALSE)
```
:::


## You Try!

Using the `anes_first200` data, create a scatter plot:

-   Put feeling towards Biden (`ft_biden`) on the **x-axis**
-   Put feeling towards Trump (`ft_trump`) on the **y-axis**
-   Use **color** to show who the voter voted for (`votechoice`)

**Take 2 minutes to try this!**

*Hint: Start with the template...*


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = ______,
       mapping = aes(x = ______, y = ______, color = ______)) +
    geom_point()
```
:::


## Solution


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = anes_first200,
       mapping = aes(x = ft_biden,
                     y = ft_trump,
                     color = votechoice)) +
    geom_point()
```
:::


## Solution


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410_DataViz_Probability_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## Now make it nicer

Add a title and meaningful axis labels:


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410_DataViz_Probability_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-13-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## Mutate

Maybe we want to know how much a voter prefers Joe Biden to Donald Trump.

An easy way to do that is to create a new column `biden_minus_trump` that is calculated by subtracting a voter's feeling thermometer score for Donald Trump from their score for Joe Biden.

We can do this using `mutate`:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
anes_first200 <- anes_first200 |>
  mutate(biden_minus_trump = ft_biden - ft_trump)
```
:::


## One Way to Plot This


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
## Basic Histogram of Biden - Trump affect
anes_first200 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = biden_minus_trump)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 10, fill = "blue") +
  labs(
    title = "Biden minus Trump Thermometer Ratings",
    x = "Feeling Thermometer Difference (Biden - Trump)",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```
:::


## One Way to Plot This


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410_DataViz_Probability_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-16-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## Another way to plot it


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
## create party names labels, drop NAs
anes_first200 <- anes_first200 |>
  mutate(party_name = ifelse(party == 1, "Democrats",
                             ifelse(party == 2, "Republicans", "Other/Independent"))) |>
  filter(is.na(party_name) == FALSE)
```
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
## Create a violin style plot displaying Biden - Trump affect
anes_first200 |>
  ggplot(aes(x = as.factor(party_name), y = biden_minus_trump)) +
  geom_violin(fill = "blue", alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.2, fill = "white", color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Biden-Trump Difference by Party ID",
    x = "Party ID",
    y = "Biden - Trump"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```
:::


## Another way to plot it


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410_DataViz_Probability_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-19-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## When Should You Use Bar Plots?

Bar plots are best for **categorical data**—when you want to compare counts or summary statistics across groups.

::: incremental
-   **Counts:** How many observations in each category?
    -   "How many respondents identify as Democrat, Republican, Independent?"
-   **Summaries:** What's the average/total for each group?
    -   "What's the average income by education level?"
-   **Comparisons:** How do groups differ?
    -   "Which department has the highest budget?"
:::

## Bar Plots vs. Histograms

| Bar Plot                                | Histogram                         |
|-----------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Categorical** X-axis (groups, labels) | **Continuous** X-axis (numbers)   |
| Bars don't touch (discrete categories)  | Bars touch (continuous range)     |
| Compare across groups                   | Show distribution of one variable |

. . .

**Rule of thumb:**

-   If X is categories/labels → Bar plot
-   If X is a continuous number → Histogram

## Which Bar Geom Should I Use?

| Function | Use When... | Y-axis shows... |
|----|----|----|
| `geom_bar()` | You want R to **count** categories | Count (automatic) |
| `geom_col()` | You already **calculated** a summary stat | Your pre-computed value |
| `geom_histogram()` | You have **continuous** data | Count in bins |

## Which Bar Geom should I use?

::: incremental
-   `geom_bar()`= "Count how many of each category"
-   `geom_col()` = "I already did the math, just plot it"
-   `geom_histogram()` = "Show me the distribution"
:::

## Example: geom_bar() counts for you


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(palmerpenguins)

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = species)) +
  geom_bar() +
  ggtitle("Count of Penguins by Species") +
  labs(x = "Species", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()
```
:::


## Example: geom_bar() counts for you


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410_DataViz_Probability_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-21-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## Example: geom_col() uses your values


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# STEP 1: Calculate the summary statistic yourself
penguin_summary <- penguins |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarize(mean_bill_length = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))

# STEP 2: Plot it with geom_col()
ggplot(data = penguin_summary, aes(x = species, y = mean_bill_length)) +
  geom_col() +
  ggtitle("Average Bill Length by Species") +
  labs(x = "Species", y = "Average Bill Length (mm)") +
  theme_minimal()
```
:::


## Example: geom_col() uses your values


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410_DataViz_Probability_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-23-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::

## Quick Reference: Common ggplot additions

| What you want       | Code to add                       |
|---------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Add points          | `+ geom_point()`                  |
| Add a trend line    | `+ geom_smooth(method = "lm")`    |
| Add a title         | `+ ggtitle("Your Title")`         |
| Label axes          | `+ labs(x = "...", y = "...")`    |
| Clean theme         | `+ theme_minimal()`               |
| Color by a variable | Inside `aes()`: `color = varname` |
| Same color for all  | Inside `geom_`: `color = "blue"`  |

## ggplot2 Cheatsheet

For a comprehensive reference, download the official ggplot2 cheatsheet:

[**ggplot2 Cheatsheet (PDF)**](https://rstudio.github.io/cheatsheets/data-visualization.pdf)

::: incremental
-   Published by Posit (formerly RStudio)
-   Two pages covering all major geoms, scales, themes, and more
-   Print it out and keep it next to your computer!
-   Also available in RStudio: **Help → Cheat Sheets → Data Visualization with ggplot2**
:::

## Rendering Your Document to HTML

When you're done writing code, you need to **render** your .qmd file to create a shareable output.

::: incremental
-   Click the **Render** button (or press `Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + K`)
-   This runs all your code chunks and creates an HTML file
-   The HTML file will appear in the same folder as your .qmd file
-   Open it in any web browser to see your polished document!
:::

## Common Rendering issues:

-   If rendering fails, check for errors in your code chunks
    -   If you can't get a block to run, comment it out with `##`
-   Make sure all packages are loaded at the top
-   Make sure your data file is in the right location

# Back to Probability

## Two Ways to Sample

When we select items from a group, there are two approaches:

::: incremental
-   **With replacement:** After each pick, put the item back. You *can* pick the same item again.
    -   Example: Rolling a die multiple times—you can get the same number twice
-   **W/o replacement:** Once you pick an item, *can't* pick it again.
    -   Example: Dealing cards—once the Ace of Spades is dealt, it can't come up again
:::

. . .

**Why does this matter?** It changes how many possible outcomes exist!

## Sampling With Replacement

**Example:** You're creating a 4-digit PIN using digits 0-9.

::: incremental
-   Can you repeat digits? **Yes!** (1234 and 1111 are both valid PINs)
-   For each digit, you have 10 choices (0-9)
-   The choices are **independent**—picking 3 first doesn't affect your next pick
:::

. . .

**Formula:** If you have **n** options and make **k** choices with replacement:

$$\text{Number of outcomes} = n^k$$

**PIN example:** $10^4 = 10{,}000$ possible PINs

## Permutations and Factorial

::: incremental
-   How many different ways can we arrange the letters ABC?

    -   Let each ordering of the letters be a **permutation** (ABC is one permutation, BCA is another)

-   This is a pretty easy problem: ABC, ABC, BAC, BCA, CAB, CBA.

    -   With more elements, it would get complicated!

-   3 ways to pick the first letter, 2 ways to pick the second once the first is picked, and one way to pick the final letter. 3\*2\*1

    -   For shorthand, we write this as 3! (3 factorial).

-   Given a set of n objects, there are n! ways to arrange them
:::

## Sampling Without Replacement

**Example:** You're picking 3 students from a class of 30 to present (in order: 1st, 2nd, 3rd).

::: incremental
-   **First pick:** 30 options
-   **Second pick:** 29 options (one student already chosen)
-   **Third pick:** 28 options
:::

. . .

**Formula:** If you have **n** items and choose **k** (order matters, no replacement):

$$\text{Number of outcomes} = n \times (n-1) \times (n-2) \times \cdots \times (n-k+1)$$

. . .

**Our example:** $30 \times 29 \times 28 = 24{,}360$ ways to pick 3 presenters in order

## With vs. Without: A Comparison

|                 | With Replacement      | Without Replacement            |
|-----------------|-----------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Can repeat?** | Yes                   | No                             |
| **Formula**     | $n^k$                 | $n \times (n-1) \times \cdots$ |
| **Example**     | PIN codes, dice rolls | Dealing cards, picking teams   |

. . .

**Key insight:** Without replacement always gives *fewer* possible outcomes (assuming k \> 1), because your options shrink with each pick.

## Let's work through an example

![](images/Birthday.png){fig-align="center"}

What is the probability that two people in this class share a birthday?

## Plotting the Birthday Problem


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410_DataViz_Probability_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-24-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## Adjusting for over counting

How many ways are there to chose a three person committee from five people?

. . .

List them all out (123)(124)(125)(134)(135)(145)(234)(235)(245)(345). So, there are **10** ways to form this committee.

. . .

or, we can use the multiplication rule. There are 5 ways to chose spot 1, 4 ways to chose spot 2, 2 ways to chose spot 3....but this *over counts because order does not matter.* A committee of Tom, Jane and Raul is the same as a committee of Rual, Jane and Tom.

## Binomial Coefficients

This leads to a more general concept - the *Binomial Coefficient* $n\choose k$ or "n choose k". This is the number of subsets of size k for a set of size n.

. . .

$$
 \frac{(n)(n-1)(n-2)....(n-k+1)}{k!}
$$

if k \< n

. . .

Equivalently

$$\frac{n!}{(n-k)!k!}$$

## Example

Suppose we need to pick 4 members of the 15 person City Council to form a select committee. How many possible different committee combinations exist?

. . .

n = 15, k = 4. (we could say, 15 choose 4) . . . So this can be written as

$$
\frac{15!}{11!*4!}
$$

## Counting Rules Summary

|                         |  **Order Matters**   | **Order Doesn't Matter** |
|-------------------------|:--------------------:|:------------------------:|
| **With Replacement**    |        $n^k$         |  $\binom{n + k - 1}{k}$  |
| **Without Replacement** | $\dfrac{n!}{(n-k)!}$ |      $\binom{n}{k}$      |

## The Basic Rules of Probability

Every probability must follow three simple rules:

::: incremental
1.  **Probabilities are between 0 and 1**
    -   P(A) = 0 means "impossible"
    -   P(A) = 1 means "certain"
2.  **Something has to happen**
    -   The probabilities of all possible outcomes add up to 1
3.  **"Or" means add (for mutually exclusive events)**
    -   If A and B can't both happen: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B)
    -   Example: P(roll a 1 OR roll a 2) = 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/6
:::

## Two More Useful Rules

**Complement Rule:** P(not A) = 1 - P(A)

-   If there's a 30% chance of rain, there's a 70% chance of no rain

. . .

**General Addition Rule:** P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)

-   We subtract P(A and B) to avoid double-counting when events can overlap

. . .

**Example:** In a class, 60% like coffee, 50% like tea, 30% like both.

P(coffee OR tea) = 0.6 + 0.5 - 0.3 = 0.8 (80% like at least one)

# Conditional Probability

## Conditioning

Conditional probability is the heart of modern statistics and social science. Getting a firm grasp on conditional probability is incredibly important if you will be making decisions based on data!

. . .

**Unconditional Probability:** What is the probability that A occurs?

. . .

**Conditional Probability:** If we know B has occurred, what is the probability that A occurs (not necessarily sequential)?

. . .

We *condition* our estimate of A on B having occurred.

. . .

Could spend a whole semester on conditional probability and fun examples.

## Conditioning is the whole game!

::: incremental
-   What is the probability that a child goes to college conditional on being *treated* by some policy intervention?

-   What is the difference in probability of being hospitalized for COVID among vaccinated and unvaccinated populations?

-   Are citizens more likely to vote if they are reminded that their decision to vote, or not vote, is visible to everyone?

-   Nearly all of social science is comparing probabilities (or means or other values) between different conditions!
:::

## Drawing Cards

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](images/Cards.jpg){fig-align="center"}
:::

:::: {.column width="50%"}
::: incremental
-   Consider a standard 52 card deck. Let A be the event of drawing a Spade and B be the event of drawing a red card.

-   What is P(A)?

-   What is P(B)?

-   What is P(A\|B)?

-   What is P(B\|A)?
:::
::::
::::::

## Definition

If $P(B)$ \> 0, then we define the conditional probability of A given B as:

$$
P(A|B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} 
$$

. . .

How often A *and* B jointly occur, divided by how often *B* occurs. Why do we need to divide by B?

## Example

What do we think $P(\text{Policy Nerd} | \text{Political Science PhD})$ is?

. . .

What about $P(\text{Political Science PhD}|\text{Policy Nerd})$?

. . .

Often assumed that $P(\text{MPA Political Science PhD|Policy Nerd})$ should also be high. This is referred to as the *base rate fallacy**.*** Most people, even policy nerds, don't get advanced degrees. And even of those who do, many get their degrees in some other, related, field like Economics. Or maybe they get a POST PhD instead.

## Weather

Let A = Presence of clouds and B = Rain

. . .

What is $P(A|B)$? (Don't over-think it - we don't need math here!)

. . .

Does that mean $P(B|A)$ is 1 too?

. . .

What about $P(B|A^{c})$?

## Senate example

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & \textbf{Democrats} & \textbf{Republicans} & \textbf{Independents} & \textbf{Total} \\
\hline
\textbf{Men} & 33 & 40 & 2 & 75 \\
\hline
\textbf{Women} & 15 & 9 & 1 & 25 \\
\hline
\textbf{Total} & 48 & 49 & 3 & 100 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

Choose one senator at random from this population. What is the probability a randomly selected Democrat is a woman?

. . .

What is the probability that a randomly selected woman is a Republican?

## Independence and Sampling

Suppose that the current prevalence of the Influenza in South Carolina is 1.5%.

. . .

If we sample 20 random people, what is the likelihood that at least one has Influenza?

. . .

What if the first person we sample has Influenza - what is the probability at least one of the remaining people has Influenza?

. . .

Note - sampling *without* replacement is independent. Sampling *with* replacement adds dependency. If N is very large, dependency is very slight.

## Conditional Independence

Two things can be **independent overall** but **related once you condition on something else**.

. . .

**Example:** Height and vocabulary are independent in the adult population.

But among 5-year-olds to 18-year-olds? Taller kids tend to have bigger vocabularies!

. . .

**Why?** Once we condition on "being a school-age child," both height and vocabulary are related to a third variable: **age**.

. . .

This concept becomes very important in regression—sometimes controlling for a variable *creates* a relationship that wasn't there before!

## Conditioning Can Create Spurious Relationships

**The College Admissions Example:**

In the general population, GPA and athletic ability are probably independent—smart kids aren't systematically better or worse at sports.

. . .

But what about **among admitted students** at a selective public university (in SC?) that values both academics and athletics?

. . .

Among admitted students, we might see a *negative* correlation

. . .

**Why?** To get admitted, you need to be strong in *something*. Conditioning on admission creates a spurious negative relationship.

## Random Variables

::: incremental
-   A **random variable** is just a number whose value depends on some random process

-   We don't know what value it will take until the process happens

-   Examples: the number of heads in 4 coin flips, a candidate's vote share, next month's unemployment rate

-   We use random variables to describe uncertainty quantitatively
:::

## An Example - Polling

::: incremental
-   Each poll is an event where **X**(poll) is % approval for President Trump.

-   Imagine polls coming from a *distribution* centered around the true support.

-   In practice, we don't know the true level of support. **X** is then the sample mean of Trump approval in each poll.

    -   This is the core of what FiveThirtyEight (RIP) and similar are doing for their election forecasts.

        -   What true level of support is *most likely* given the observed polling data (plus some other factors).
:::

## Types of Random Variables

::: incremental
-   **Discrete**: Can only take specific, countable values
    -   Number of students absent, vote counts, survey responses (1-5)
-   **Continuous**: Can take any value in a range
    -   Income, temperature, time, test scores
-   This distinction matters for choosing the right statistical method later
:::

## Distributions: Describing Uncertainty

::: incremental
-   A **distribution** tells us how likely each possible value is

-   Example: If X = number of days a student was absent

    -   What's the chance a student missed more than 10 days?
    -   What's the chance they had perfect attendance?

-   The distribution answers these questions by showing the probability of each outcome
:::

## Simple Example

Consider flipping 4 fair coins, where **X** is the number of heads.

. . .

What is P(X = 1)?

. . .

What about P(X = 2 or 3)?

. . .

What about P(X \> 4)?

## Distribution of X


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410_DataViz_Probability_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-25-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## Distributions in Practice: Experimental Design

Imagine you are designing an experimental educational policy intervention with 3 treatment conditions (Control, T1 and T2).

. . .

Good randomization is the foundation of experimental inference, so you decide to randomize into conditions by flipping four coins. Let **X** be the number of heads. If **X** is 0 or 1 you assign the school to the control. If **X** is 2 you assign it to T1, and if **X** \>2, you assign it to T2.

. . .

What does the distribution of **X** look like? Would you use this randomization technique?

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
